Lunajson.input_format: description of expected arguments (types, names, optionality)
Lunajson.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
Lunajson.description: Lunajson is a SAX-style JSON parser and simple JSON decoder/encoder. It is tested on Lua 5.1, Lua 5.2, Lua 5.3, Lua 5.4, and LuaJIT 2.1. It is written only in pure Lua and has no dependencies. Even so, decoding speed matches lpeg-based JSON implementations because it is carefully optimized. The parser and decoder reject input that is not conformant to the JSON specification (ECMA-404), and the encoder always yields conformant output. The parser and decoder also handle UTF/Unicode surrogate pairs correctly.

global_func.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
global_func.description: This function encodes a table `t` as a JSON array if a value `t[1]` is present or a number `t[0]` is present. If `t[0]` is present, its value is considered as the length of the array. Then the array may contain `nil` and those will be encoded as `null`. Otherwise, this function scans non-`nil` values starting from index 1, up to the first `nil` it finds. If this constraint is not met or unsupported types (e.g., function) are contained in `value`, an error will be raised.

upload_servers.description: A list of aliases for URLs, to be used for accessing repositories through various protocols. Each entry has a string alias as a key and a table as a value. This table value contains protocol names as keys and pathnames as values. Protocols "http", "ftp", and "sftp" are supported. Example: `{ rocks = { http = "www.example.com/rocks", sftp = "example.com/var/rocks" } }`.

arch.description: A two-part string identifying operating system and hardware architecture, for filtering binary rocks. This value is autodetected by LuaRocks. Example values are 'macosx-powerpc' and 'win32-x86'.

platforms.description: A list of string identifiers indicating which platform constraints can be satisfied by the running system. This is used for filtering commands on the LuaRocks build rules. This allows a more general platform definition such as 'unix' when the same build commands are valid for all Unix variants, instead of enumerating all known valid arch entries, and at the same time using a more specific definition such as 'macosx' when a platform-specific flag is used. This value is automatically filled according to the value of _arch_.

luarocks.new_version.description: Creates a rockspec for a new version of a rock based on data from an existing rockspec. If a package name is given, it downloads the latest rockspec from the public rocks server. If a rockspec URL or path is given, it uses it instead. If no argument is given, it looks for a rockspec same way the luarocks make command does. New version of the rockspec can be specified by passing it as the second argument or by using --tag option: the tag will be used as version, with leading 'v' removed. New URL (value of source.url rockspec field) can be passed as the third argument. If it's not passed explicitly, it will be inferred from the old URL by replacing occurrences of the old version in it with the new one. If new tag (value of source.tag) is not set explicitly using --tag, it is updated in the same way.

make.add_to_parser.input_format: parser:command("make", [[Builds sources in the current directory, but unlike "build", it does not fetch sources, etc., assuming everything is available in the current directory. If no argument is given, it looks for a rockspec in the current directory and in "rockspec/" and "rockspecs/" subdirectories, picking the rockspec with newest version or without version name. If rockspecs for different rocks are found or there are several rockspecs without version, you must specify which to use, through the command-line. This command is useful as a tool for debugging rockspecs. To install rocks, you'll normally want to use the "install" and "build" commands. See the help on those for details. If the current directory contains a luarocks.lock file, it is used as the authoritative source for exact version of dependencies. The --pin flag overrides and recreates this file scanning dependency based on ranges.]], util.see_also())

cjson.encode_empty_table_as_object.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.encode_empty_table_as_object.description: Change the default behavior when encoding an empty Lua table. By default, empty Lua tables are encoded as empty JSON Objects. If this is set to false, empty Lua tables will be encoded as empty JSON Arrays instead.

cjson.empty_array.input_format: description of expected arguments (types, names, optionality)
cjson.empty_array.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.empty_array.description: A lightuserdata, similar to `cjson.null`, which will be encoded as an empty JSON Array by `cjson.encode()`. For example, since `encode_empty_table_as_object` is `true` by default, `cjson.empty_array` will be encoded as `[]`.

cjson.array_mt.input_format: description of expected arguments (types, names, optionality)
cjson.array_mt.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.array_mt.description: When lua-cjson encodes a table with this metatable, it will systematically encode it as a JSON Array. The resulting, encoded Array will contain the array part of the table, and will be of the same length as the `#` operator on that table. Holes in the table will be encoded with the `null` JSON value.

cjson.empty_array_mt.input_format: description of expected arguments (types, names, optionality)
cjson.empty_array_mt.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.empty_array_mt.description: A metatable which can 'tag' a table as a JSON Array in case it is empty (that is, if the table has no elements). Instead of `local function serialize(arr)`, this is more concise: `local function serialize(arr) setmetatable(arr, cjson.empty_array_mt) return cjson.encode({some_array = arr}) end`.

cjson.encode_number_precision.input_format: description of expected arguments (types, names, optionality)
cjson.encode_number_precision.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.encode_number_precision.description: This fork allows encoding of numbers with a `precision` up to 16 decimals (vs. 14 in mpx/lua-cjson).

cjson.encode_escape_forward_slash.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.encode_escape_forward_slash.description: If enabled, forward slash '/' will be encoded as '\/' in JSON. If disabled, forward slash '/' will be encoded as '/' (no escape is applied).

cjson.encode_skip_unsupported_value_type.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.encode_skip_unsupported_value_type.description: If enabled, cjson will not throw exception when there are unsupported types in the Lua table. For example, `cjson.encode_skip_unsupported_value_types(true)` will allow encoding of unsupported types.

cjson.encode_indent.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.encode_indent.description: If non-empty string provided, JSON values encoded by `cjson.encode()` will be formatted in a human-readable way, using `indent` for indentation at each nesting level. Also enables newlines and a space after colons.

cjson.decode_array_with_array_mt.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.decode_array_with_array_mt.description: If enabled, JSON Arrays decoded by `cjson.decode` will result in Lua tables with the `array_mt` metatable. This can ensure a 1-to-1 relationship between arrays upon multiple encoding/decoding of your JSON data with this module.

cjson.decode_allow_comment.output_format: description of return values (types, structure, or multiple returns)
cjson.decode_allow_comment.description: If enabled, allows JavaScript-style comments in `cjson.decode` input. Comments are treated as whitespace and may appear anywhere whitespace is valid in JSON. Supports single-line comments beginning with '//' and block comments enclosed with '/* ... */'.
